content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I am obsessed with the movie Amélie…

**Score:** 62
**Body:**  I love the storytelling, colors and quirky characters. Anyone know of anything similar that just leaves you feeling happy inside?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Chungking Express 
2. For whimsy and vintage/pretty visuals, you might enjoy Wes Anderson’s films 
3. Triplets of Belleville 
4. Young Girls of Rochefort

Not a movie, but Pushing Daisies. 
5. A Very Long Engagement, Chocolat, Delicatessan
6. Regarding Amelie, Me too!! It’s a darker and more violent path to get there, but I’d suggest Dust Bunny. Same guy as Pushing Daisies (which was also marvelous). In terms of that “feeling”, do you like animation? If so, I’d also recommend Flow.
7. Sabrina (new or old version), Midnight in Paris, Big Fish. 
8. Princess Bride. A bit old but definitely makes you happy inside. 

As for a similarly unique approach as Amelie you can try 

Love me if you dare 

Big fish 


9. Taika Waititi movies. Bit heavier, but quirky and colorful.
10. Cinema Paradiso. It’s a bit heavier but the payoff to the plot left me feeling similarly light in spirit.

## 2. (1995) Tank Girl... movie was wild... surprised there was not a sequel or even a reboot/remake...

**Score:** 50
**Body:** &#x200B;

The movie is very much what the movie title suggests except that she is technically a woman with a Tank.

So "Tank Woman".

Movie was wild and had lots of massive predictions about the future. 

If they make a movie sequel then they could do...  "Submarine Woman"...

or if they do a reboot/remake then I think that Margot Robbie or Sarah Sherman would be funny. 

The original actress should also make a cameo. She was a good actress.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The artist that did the comics is one half of Gorrilaz.
  - This is the 3rd Gorillaz reference I've seen in 8 hours. 

Reference to them being on Saturday Night Live. 

Reference to an artistic choice resembling their art. 

Now this. 

Kinda thinking this is a VERY effective PR move. 
    - Gorrillaz have been around for over 20 years.

They just released a new album.

They are a part of popular culture.

Their experiment worked.
2. I mean Tank Girl is an actual comic?

You can't really make a sequel that doesn't follow the direction of the comic
  - The comic was amazing. 
  - bruh in comic book world there is nothing that can't not be done, can't be undone, ignored, changed, or just exploded with dynamite.


  - The Walking Dead would like to have a word
3. I think the problem was it was based on a fairly obscure independent comic. It didn’t have a big enough fan base to justify a sequel.

It was good to see Lori Petty playing the Conductor of The Travelling Symphony in Station 11. 
4. It bombed critically and financially, that's why. Gained a cult following. It's enjoyable in its weirdness.
5. It's bombed at The Box Office. Typically, studio execs aren't going to invest in a sequel if they lost money on the 1st movie.

## 3. Sci Fi movie fans, what movie would you recommend to someone get lost in? What movies would you recommend to someone who doesn't watch sci fi movies?

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I need sci fi movie recs from people who are really into the genre. What sci fi movies should I watch if I want to become obsessed with the genre? I've seen Dune and Star Wars already, so you don't have to worry about those. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Gattaca.   
Upgrade.   
Moon.

Paul Verhoven's satire trio of Robocop, Total Recall and Starship Troopers

Terminator and Terminator 2

Alien, Aliens, Predator, The Thing

Star Trek is it's own enourmous thing, but you can watch The Wrath of Kahn or First Contact entirely on their own as just good movies.

The Back to the Future trilogy

Understand that a really good sci-fi often isn't telling us something about the future, but about now
2. 1) annihilation 
2) ex machina
3) 12 monkeys
4) fifth element 
5) gattica
3. Enemy mine
4. Moon
5. *”Alien”* (1979)

*”Aliens”* (1986)

*”Arrival”* (2016)

*”Gravity”* (2013)

*”The Martian”* (2015)
6. Alien.  It will always be Alien.

7. Blade Runner movies

District 9

A Scanner Darkly 

The Matrix 

Johnny Mnemonic 


8. Arrival

Alien

Europa Report

Pacific Rim

Jason X

Terminator 1 & 2

Contact

2001: A Space Odyssey

Star Trek: The Wrath of Khan

  
Edit: speeling
9. Children of men
10. are you looking for sci-fi movies or more movies like dune and star wars? those two are more fantasy/space opera than sci-fi.

## 4. Movies with the best car scenes

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Looking for movies with the best car scenes. It can be a car chase or just a scene that takes place in a car. 

An example to describe both of those categories would be that one scene from *Before the devil knows you're dead (2007)* where Phillip Seymour Hoffman's character starts breaking down in the car. 

The other example would best fit something like the car chase scene from *The French Connection (1971).*

If possible, please refrain from repeating any suggestions already mentioned in the comments and thanks in advance for commenting. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bullitt (1968)
2. The Blues Brothers 1980
3. Ronin 
4. Baby driver (2017)
5. Mad Max Fury road
6. One Battle After Another had some fun car scenes
7. The Bourne Identity (2002) Matt Damon driving a beat up Mini Cooper through Paris' narrow alleys, down steep steps...
8. Children Of Men

First of all, amazing futuristic apocalyptic but very recognizable car design,  
Second, awesome long take scene in a car chase / chased car.
9. Duel (1971), based on a short story by Richard Matheson and directed by Steven Spielberg. Not your traditional ‘car chase’ scenes, but still one of the most tension filled movies out there.
10. Drive (2011)

## 5. Please go see the 1967 Casino Royale

**Score:** 18
**Body:** You should watch the modern Casino Royale first, because you will need it to even begin to comprehend the psychedelic madness that is the '67 Casino Royale. It's absolute fever-dream of a film.

Let me be straight, the movie is bad, but it's a kind of bad even beyond something like The Room, which is sincerely bad through genuine incompetence. The 67 Casino Royale has an absolute battallion of top-flight talent behind it, in front and behind the cameras, and it uses that talent on some of the lamest writing you can even concieve of. It almost rises to the level of a strange sort of performance art, like if Daniel Day Lewis and Meryl Streep starred in a shot-for-shot remake of Freddie Got Fingered directed by Paul Thomas Anderson. 

It's a movie that defies good sense, defies comprehension. How did this get pushed through production hell? How did this ever see the light of day? I don't know, but the fact that it did is reason enough to see it, and cherish it for a slice of absolute lunacy. 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You had me at Freddie Got Fingered
2. Follow up. In the mood to see a bad "Bond like" ripoff? Watch "Our Man Flynt" with James Coburn. 
So bad it's good. 
  - Why stop there?

How I Won the War (1967)

British satire during the the Summer of Love is fucking awful 
3. Love this zany movie!

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What’s a simple line that rips your heart to shreds?

**Score:** 1218
**Body:** In frame: Manchester By The Sea

I mean, the entire film is basically a test of your emotional strength and in the end, you lose that battle anyway. 

But this scene is the ultimate destruction after what feels like a series of heartbreaks. And it’s a very simple (one-line) dialogue which is the last straw. 

Which makes me wonder, what are some of the simplest lines in films that just leave you heartbroken? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Where are his glasses??  He can’t see without his glasses?


2. https://preview.redd.it/yo0rhti4cuqg1.png?width=1159&format=png&auto=webp&s=fd5d9a0e5d42e60c6a34ad96872784428da65823


  - When he asks to not have the bag on his head cause he’s afraid of the dark……..that gets me
3. "I could have got one more, and I didn't!"

Schindler's List
4. ![gif](giphy|OJmCYj6KNYlSE)
5. https://preview.redd.it/k1ypveuhbuqg1.png?width=1400&format=png&auto=webp&s=b5f6b2e313bce2ac8240107d8c8eed8d4f4247d8


6. https://preview.redd.it/6p51mp2xbuqg1.jpeg?width=736&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=6c2a8e49fde38a1b176faf99a486e453a645d243

“That I’m not a mistake” gets me every time.
7. Forrest Gump seeing his son for the first time:

"Is he smart, or...?"
8. Manchester by the sea has a lot of these. “There’s nothin’ there”
9. ![gif](giphy|14nEjMEbnUkFvq|downsized)

## 2. What are the best “ride or die” moments in film?

**Score:** 677
**Body:** I mean those scenes where a character backs their friend no matter what. Jeremy Renner’s “Jem” in The Town (2010) doesn’t ask questions when Ben Affleck’s Doug asks for help.

“I need your help. I can’t tell you what it is, you can never ask me about it later, and we’re gonna hurt some people.”

“Whose car are we gonna take?”

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hell or Highwater. “Because you asked little brother”
2. Come on, Mr. Frodo. I can’t carry it for you. But I can carry you. 
  - My favourite version of this

https://preview.redd.it/tdmz7e81utqg1.jpeg?width=357&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=b8b1c22df9c09585d4e7e757d379607517697a55
3. Lethal Weapon 4  
"I mean, he took my gun apart with one deft move. How did he do that? Huh? How?"  

"....Yeah, okay. Let's go ask him."
4. "That's my brother right there. What did you do to get him so cranked up, huh?"
5. In Crazy Stupid Love, the second Ryan Gosling's character meets David Lindhagen at the end of the movie he immediately walks up to him and sucker punches him on behalf of his friend.  


  - It’s not a sucker punch, he addressed him by name, looked into his eyes, and took off his ring before decking that stranger!
6. This one from The Town felt particularly descended from the young Tommy Lee Jones moment in Rolling Thunder. 

After visiting his old war buddy, and sitting down for small talk with his family, the protagonist takes him aside:

“I found them.”

“Who?”

“The men who killed my son.”

[“…I’ll just get my gear.”](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=X3fKfAWUgdA&pp=ygUgcm9sbGluZyB0aHVuZGVyIGknbGwgZ2V0IG15IGdlYXI%3D)

7. A show not a movie, but one of my favorite small moments is in Roseanne, when they find out Jackie has been beaten by her boyfriend. Roseanne is talking to her and Jackie is crying, when Dan walks in the room. He asks what happened and Roseanne says "Jackie's boyfriend hit her" before taking her to the other room. 

Dan doesn't say anything. Grabs his coat, and leaves.
  - And calmly comes home, cracks a beer, and waits to be arrested.

## 3. What did everyone think of The Martian?

**Score:** 370
**Body:** I watched it today for the first time and I LOVED it. It’s a masterpiece on every level. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I very much enjoyed it. Good adaptation of the source material as well, I think. 
  - There’s something depression healing about watching someone be dropped into an impossible no win scenario and instead of throw in the towel instead problem solve the hell out there way out of the problem. White boards, dump everything you have out on the table roll up your sleeves and get to work.

The book Hail Mary felt the exact same way.

2. They call it a competency film and it is. People pulling together to solve one problem after another in a tightly entertaining movie. 

9/10
  - Its especially refreshing with such tense action packed movies without an actual villain. Just people tackling shit that gets thrown at them. 
3. Loved it. Read the book twice and I’ve watched the movie five times minimum.

Headed to Project Hail Mary on Wednesday and looking so forward to it
4. Matt Damon 
  - Matt Damon
    - ![gif](giphy|rqGWkh4eLfaM)
5. In a time where every nations is hostile against each other, it’s nice to see how united the countries are in the movie to save one guy, even if that’s probably never gonna happen in our future, I appreciate when telling a story with a sense of optimism 
6. I thought it was super solid. I don’t know if I’d call it a masterpiece, but it was pretty good. I’d give it a 7.5/10.

The main complaint I remember having was that Matt Damon’s character seemed way too nonchalant about the whole situation. He had a few moments of real anger/frustration/fear, but not enough for the situation he was in IMO.

## 4. Project Hail Mary is what people have been saying about Interstellar for years

**Score:** 364
**Body:** I disagreed with a lot of the praise given to *Interstellar*. I still think it’s a great film in many ways (I gave it a 4/5), but putting it in the conversation for best movie of the decade—let alone of all time—feels excessive.

I just got back from watching *Project Hail Mary*, and yeah, it was amazing. Not quite a 5/5 for me since it didn’t fully hit that “wow” factor, but still very strong. What stood out most, though, is how it handles many of the elements people often praise *Interstellar* for—and, in my opinion, does them better.

I do get that these movies are quite different in a lot of ways, but the parallels are still there and all the praise I heard for Interstellar fits this movie so perfectly.

The story feels much more human. The characters are well thought out and properly developed, without sidelining anyone when their role actually matters. The main character, especially, is written far more effectively.

The science also comes across as more believable. I don’t need perfect realism, but considering how much *Interstellar* gets praised for its scientific accuracy, *Project Hail Mary* fits that praise more naturally.

Visually, it’s also more appealing. The spectacle is on par with *Interstellar*, but the more vibrant and colorful presentation elevates it beyond the latter’s muted style.

I was also more emotionally invested this time. The stakes felt real, and the pacing stayed consistent and engaging throughout. And importantly, it sticks the landing. The ending is hopeful and feel-good, but it actually feels earned and logical—there’s no moment where you think, “they really shouldn’t have survived that.”

What really ties it all together is the tone. *Project Hail Mary* manages to balance the seriousness of its premise with humor and warmth. It can be a bit corny at times, but it’s done with enough sincerity that it never feels out of place.

There is also a thing of Interstellar taking itself too seriously at times that the dialogue fail to land while PHM tone and energy just allows it to do everything well at the same time.

I genuinely would much prefer PHM replacing Interstellar's placement on Letterboxd.

EDIT: Ok I'd like to address one thing that is often brought up in the comments. I understand that the post inherently brings comparisons even though these are 2 vastly different films. That said, the thing I was trying to get at was the particular things that were praised about Interstellar hit me as very fitting for PHM. And when comparing those specific parts, I just thought PHM executed things better. Now I do admit that I wrote all of that post with a very hazy memory of Interstellar tainted by my distaste for the ending and the "force of love" message. Feel free to show your disagreement, this is reddit! People cannot convince me that PHM was just some dumb Marvel-esque flick with mediocre writing for babies. I am starting to consider rewatching Interstellar tho.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's nothing like Interstellar. And that's not a bad thing. Both movies are great.
  - OP seems to be karma farming. They keep posting this to other subs like TrueFilm. 

Feels like a marketing tactic to compare the 2 films. 
    - the odds are higher that OP just craves validation and can only get it from the internet. 
  - i think its odd to say its nothing like interstellar. They touch on similar themes with similar plot points, and PHM is the only movie recently that can match Interstellar in its grandiose visuals as well as emotional impact. I think both are great, but I also came away mostly wanting to discuss the ways in which they're similar and different. 
2. Interesting how different people perceive things. Pretty much every point you said, where you think Project Hail Mary is better, I think Interstellar was better.
  - I love Project Hail Mary, as a book and as a movie. But in no way is it better than Interstellar. 
3. These movies may be in the same genre but they are so completely different in tone and execution that it feels unfair to compare them.

It’s like saying Rush Hour is everything Lethal Weapon wanted to be.
4. > without sidelining anyone when their role actually matters


I really don't agree with this one. In fact this is a film which completely sidelines many relevant side characters to the point they become a total non entity.


The supporting characters are probably the weakest part of the book anyway, but they have an important role in the backstory that gets completely sidelined. Even Stratt has her story heavily cut down, many people don't even really understand what her role was besides being the main characters boss.
5. Both films are great but could not disagree more with your opinion. I prefer a more serious / gritty tone with a film. Project Hail Mary was just a massive crowd pleaser
6. Disagree. Loved both. They're different stories and that's okay 

## 5. Cannot believe I have slept on this for so long.

**Score:** 329
**Body:** I have been meaning to watch this one for a good while now.
Finally got around to it and boy was it an entertaining experience!
Sam Rockwell is such a versatile actor, the entire movie I was engaged. Really interesting story and some genuinely good writing.

If you haven't seen it yet, do yourself a favour and watch it!

That is all I have to say. 

Until next time!

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Definitely a fun watch! Rockwell doesn’t miss
2. I rented this on LoveFilm (the UK’s answer to Netflix back when Netflix posted DVDs) because I had a rental left that month and it was on the front page. No other reason.   

Blew me away. 
Duncan Jones was on amazing form, then followed up with the excellent Source Code. 
  - Source Code is criminally underrated. Such a solid movie.. but I do kinda feel bad about the ending lol
3. Every now and then, I will have a Sam Rockwell day.. where I watch some of my favorites as eye bleach.

1. Moon
2. Galaxy Quest
3. Choke
4. Seven Psychopaths
5. The Way Way Back

Are among my most rewatched
  - If you haven’t seen Mr. Right, I’d add it to the list. Also Hitchhikers Guide to the Galaxy
    - Oh, Hitchiker's!! And I haven't seen Mr. Right. I'll check it out
  - Add "good luck, have fun, don't die"
New and definitely top 5 for rockwell
4. I love the Moon, it feels very realistic in showing that we would need bioengineered humans for space travel. The human body simply isn’t evolved for that environment, and most science fiction tends to ignore this fact.

I would also recommend a film called Ink once you have recovered from emotional experience of moon.
  - Co-written by Duncan Jones and Nathan Parker. Jones is David Bowie’s son.
5. Yeah man, you should try a mattress. 

Awesome movie tho! 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. I find aspect ratio changes to (mostly) be incredibly distracting

**Score:** 128
**Body:** There was recently a video of an IMAX projectionist filming the trailer for Dune: Part Two, and everyone was understandably in awe at how epic the IMAX shots look. With that said, I personally found it jarring to see the aspect ratio change every other shot. Of course, it feels even more jarring in a trailer, but I still feel pretty similar when I watch a feature length movie. At the end of the day, the feeling I want to experience when watching most movies is for the frame to disappear, and to feel truly immersed. But when a movie is 50% IMAX and 50% non-IMAX, it becomes distracting in how much it draws attention to itself.

For me, aspect ratio changes work best when they don’t feel arbitrary. A good example of this is Project Hail Mary, which exclusively uses IMAX aspect ratio for the space scenes, while the Earth scenes are not in IMAX aspect ratio, which makes sense when you consider the vastness of space. But when I watch something like Dune, even though I think the movies are brilliant, the aspect ratio changes draw attention to themselves and feels like the director ‘picking favourites’ from scene-to-scene. Yet, there’s nothing more distracting than being able to tell that a scene just switched out of IMAX because the characters are now talking quietly, and the IMAX camera is too loud for that particular scene. Is that something you personally feel or do you have a different experience with it?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Transformers 5, aside from being a genuinely horrendous film in itself, was such a tough watch with the aspect ratio literally changing every few seconds.

As for IMAX, I don’t mind as long as it’s not happening within the same scene. If it goes to IMAX for an entire sequence, that’s fine by me. 
2. The coolest aspect ratio change I’ve seen so far is the one from the home release of Mission: Impossible: Fallout (the franchise with the record for the most colons in the movie titles.) The movie is in standard up until we get to the point where Ethan and Walker are preparing for the big HALO jump over Paris. We follow Ethan’s POV as he walks to the rear of the plane and looks out of the open bay, and as he is looking over the edge, that aspect ratio gradually expands to the top and bottom of the screen, and it just feels like you’re looking into this yawning abyss. Great stuff, thoughtfully implemented.
  - I believe they did this in a couple of the late MI films (slowly expand the aspect ratio for the one or two scenes that use it), even in the theater
    - yeah they did this in final reckoning when he opens the hatch on the submarine
3. It’s one thing to have different scenes switch aspect ratios. You notice it but forget. Dark knight rises cuts back and forth shot to shot and it’s distracting to the point where I’m like, “what is this fucking nonsense? Call me when it stops.”
4. >For me, aspect ratio changes work best when they don’t feel arbitrary.

What do you think of The Grand Budapest Hotel, which uses different aspect ratios to signify the three different time periods in the narratives? That's an example of a very motivated, non-arbitrary use of this technique.
5. I think it has been used in fairly novel and interesting ways before, particularly by Xavier Dolan and Trey Edward Schultz. 

Their changes in aspect ratio are actually attached to the emotions of their stories, which I think is personally the way to go when it comes to multiple aspect ratios per film. 

With IMAX I have found it distracting in some circumstances. I remember a scene in Sinners where it changes between shots about 4 times in the span of a second. But if it’s largely sticking to one vs the other at any one time, I usually don’t mind it too much. 

Honestly I’d rather there be multiple aspect ratios in a particular film than have to deal with the horrendous, outdated practice of pan-and-scanning CinemaScope/2:35 super 35 films. Which, for some reason, is still occurring with frequency.
6. IMO, **this modern trend of switching between IMAX ratio and 2.39:1/2.76:1 Ultra Panavision (Sinners, Dune, Oppenheimer, ect) is the thing that's actually going to date movies from this period.**

I've never been a huge fan of IMAX either; it's like a slightly wider 4:3 with more DOF and more resolution than anyone could possibly need, whoopity do. For documentary stuff it was kinda cool, for narrative it just feels like a gimmick to sell more tickets.
7. The other problem with aspect ratios is it literally changes the rule of thirds meaning that your eyeline is gojng to change every time it switches.   Ultimately it means that there is no true way to watch the movie, any version is going to require compromise. It’s distracting.
8. the perfect usage is in Hunger Games: Catching Fire. as she takes the elevator up to the arena, she sees a brutal act of violence and is pounding trying to get out. while the elevator passes through the ground, the aspect ratio expands almost-invisibly and she emerges into an IMAX frame (full-frame on Blu-ray), and the arena is entirely in IMAX/full-frame from that point forward. it's a hugely emotional moment because now she's in this wide-open space and needs to snap into survival mode, with no time to process what she just saw in the "real" world.

unfortunately they've eliminated it from some releases (including the one I bought on iTunes) because people were complaining about the ratio change, but you can see it here https://youtu.be/ggqOV88WYws


## 2. Pillion (2025) did anyone else see this and enjoy?

**Score:** 16
**Body:** This film just came to local theaters in my country, and I must say I thoroughly enjoyed it. I suppose its not really relevant at all, but i'm not gay so i'm probably not the demographic for what is essentially a black-comedy "dom com". It was to me a particularly gnarly depiction of a gay sub-culture I was not aware of, however I came out of this film feeling like this film speaks to a true human experience that is universal. Must we belittle ourselves at any cost in seeking of true love and companionship? I think most people can relate to loving so deeply and passionately that it can become arguably a twisted version of itself.
I am a massive fan of black comedies in general so this was already up my alley, but I would love to hear if anyone else felt any similar experience watching Pillion. The performances were excellent, the score was melancholy and beautiful, and I truly think its my favourite film i've seen in 2026, up with the likes of Sentimental Value and Hamnet.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My partner and I saw it a couple weeks ago at the theatre and both of us really enjoyed it. I agree with you the way the movie shows how we sort twist ourselves when we've met someone we want to be with, but I liked how the main character took everything as something to learn and grow from. I also enjoyed how unapologetically horny it was. I technically won the movie tickets at work for a Valentines Day raffle, my manager asked me the other day what we ended up seeing, I said it was a dark comedy called Pillion and left it at that. 
  - Haha nice save there with your boss. I feel like its a hard one to reccomend others, unless you know they're chill of course. I saw it with my best friend and we both agreed it was it wasn't a total tragedy, and Colin regained some of his spirit and thirst for life back.
2. I saw this on Saturday night at a local theater and I also came to a similar conclusion. Kind of like how Brokeback Mountain was first and foremost a very resonant, relatable love story that also just happened to involve two dudes. Like, yeah, it was gay but it was also universal. I felt a similar feeling about Pillion. I'm a straight male and quite vanilla in my bedroom tastes so some of those scenes, whoah. A bit hard to roll with. They really 'go there.' BUT I was also left with a feeling that the film was more of a story about power dynamics in relationships, and where your own boundaries lie. How many of your wants can you go without in order to maintain a peace and compromise? What are your deal breakers? Where are your own limits? From that point of view, I found it to be very relatable and it gave me a lot to think about, even though it was quite gay and very kinky, neither of which is my personal jam. A very interesting experience to watch this film.
  - I think I had a nearly identical experience to you, it was pretty zesty and crazy but pretty funny and entertaining to watch in that regard.
3. I saw it and just meh'd out. I can see the performances are good and so on but I just couldn't relate to the relationship on a gut level.

I was thinking afterwards about why this is. Like maybe I'm homophobic or something, but then I thought more. 

I think for a love story to have it's full effect you have to be slightly in love yourself -- with one of the characters, I mean. So like lesbian romances can and usually do work on me, but man-on-man just doesn't grab me.\* 

This said, I did like aspects of it very much. Skarsgaard's character was pretty interesting and played superbly. I empathised a great deal with his fear of vulnerable intimacy, so much that the final day was rather uncomfortable for me. 

Loved the dinner scene. 

Loved the inversion of character expectations beneath the surface. The dom is actually the more sensitive, the one who needs most the safety of sub-dom etiquette to protect them. The sub appears to be in some sense victimised or abused in some minor way, but is in fact growing and learning what their needs and boundaries are.

Didn't find it very funny at all honestly. Maybe one or two moments, but I rarely find films funny so ymmv

\*I do like a lot of films in which gay shit features prominently. E.g. I gave All of Us Strangers 10/10 but it was more the parent-child relationship thing that sent me. 
  - Thats all totally fair, not everything is going to slap for everyone. I agree fully with your point regarding Skaarsgard being the most vulnerable one, that was a super interesting aspect. Also, I appreciate it didn't fully follow the trope of all homosexual stories ending in a complete tragedy for a change, it felt pretty refreshing to me.
4. It was also my favorite film I've seen recently. Fully well crafted on a scriptwriting, acting, cinematographic, and music and sound level. Honestly, I thought it was somewhat perfect so glad to see you liked it too!

I didn't really know what I was getting into either and also had a very strong emotional reaction to the film. I thought the ending was spectacularly executed. >!I feel like many lesser writers or directors would have let the film wind towards its ending when Colin takes the bike. Symbolically the power dynamic has shifted on the most key symbol of the film. Thus yadda yadda, this action leads to the end of the relationship, very heavyhandedly symbolic and an ending we could all see a mile away. HOWEVER, Ray surprises us (me anyway) with going along with the "day off" that Colin proposes and it is this version of "flying too close to the sun" that causes Ray to choose to run. It's so interesting. The film is "about Colin" in a way, his sexual coming of age, but it's also so much about Ray, his journey towards vulnerability at the service of this relationship with this rather naive newcomer to his community.!<

And I must also add that it was a pleasure for something to be so tightly plotted. Every scene builds on a question that the audience may have about the core relationships of the film. Nothing felt superfluous even though there were stylistic scenes. I felt every scene earns its place.

## 3. The Five Obstructions 2003. One of the finest documentaries on the nature of filmmaking and no one talks about it.

**Score:** 10
**Body:** The Five obstructions was a documentary film directed by Lars Von Trier in 2003 and is perhaps one of the finest meditations on the natire of filmmaking and filmakers unique creativity that’s ever been made.

In the film Lars challenges his film making mentor and hero Leth, to remake his masterpiece film 5 separate times each time with 5 different limitations.” what follows is a truly eye opening and inspiring look into creativity under pressure and what limitations can do for any act of creation.

## 5. Strange disconnect with specific end plot point in Project Hail Mary

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I've seen very little critical discourse on Project Hail Mary and one of the film's biggest reveals: 

\*SPOILER\*: the government drugs ryan gosling's character to force him to go on the space mission after he refuses. He obviously completes his mission and saves humanity (yay!), but...\*END SPOILER. 

What I find strange is the decision to reveal that twist in the last 45 minutes of the movie - with no emotional action or follow-up for the rest of the movie. Even more strange, all is seemingly forgiven by the end of the movie. Movie logic that's simply following a book released in 2021? Certainly. But, given all-time low trust in the current US administration & world powers AND how much absolute love this movie is getting from the internet (4.4 rating on Letterboxd atm) I feel like I'm the crazy one for calling out some of these things in the script and direction of the film.

  
Anyone else pickup on this specific parts of the film and feel similar? I love a feel good film, but this feels like a 3/5 feel good flick vs. the 4.5-5/5 all time classic with a truly authentic human center (Interstellar, Solaris...even the The Martian potentially) in which audiences are positioning it.

Thank you for your thoughts. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think you're missing the fact that this reveal recontextualizes his entire belief that he made this selfless decision earlier in the film. It's also there to deepen his selfless decision later to go and sacrifice his ability to return home by saving rocky.  
2. In the book, there is more narrative weight given to that realization.  But, it doesn't come from dwelling on the government's (though, it isn't really the government; its the "Project Hail Mary" team) decision, but rather Ryland interrogates his own cowardice.  Ryland also feels betrayed by Eva Stratt, specifically, rather than the organization as whole, but ultimately understands why she did that.

Ultimately Project Hail Mary (in both the book and film) is relatively uncynical and so interrogating government mistrust is not a high priority for the narrative.
  - adding to that is his emotional threat to sabotage the mission when they put him aboard by force which is also why it’s an amnesia drug 
3. I'm no film critic, but this reveal basically sets up a (somewhat retroactive) arc for Grace. Shortly after the reveal that he was too much a coward to sacrifice himself initially, he becomes brave enough to sacrifice himself for Rocky near the end. Without this reveal, Grace doesn't really have any character growth throughout the film. 
  - I think it also makes it retroactively more logical that he didn't remember volunteering (or have deep understanding of the ship functions since he received no astronaut training).   He only has a few hours from being asked to go to being placed into an induced coma.
  - That also ties in with a conversation that Grace has with Commander Lau when they're still on Earth. He talks about not having the courage or bravery to do what the Commander has volunteered to do, and the Commander essentially says he needs to find someone to be brave for. (I don't remember the exact wording, but that's the gist)

Grace had nobody on Earth, so while the world was at stake he didn't have that one person to fuel that bravery. But in Rocky, he found the one that he was willing to sacrifice himself for and be brave of his own choice. These all tie together to make that decision more impactful, in my opinion anyway.
4. In the book, it was a huge reveal at the end as well. I think it comes at the end because by then, Ryland has done a whole bunch of heroic stuff, but now we know why he thinks of himself as a coward. And he then makes the one consciously heroic choice in the end when he realizes he can do one thing or the other. Contrast that with the previous 95 % of the story where his choices were really more survival than specifically actively choosing to be a hero and save humanity.
5. I just saw PHM for the second time and this time I recognized that Grace silently having a breakdown after right seeing the inside of Rocky’s ship is because of his realization that he had actually been a coward when he was called on to step up. For some reason I didn’t quite make that connection the first time but it was clear this time around. Also him remembering his own cowardice really deepens the significance of his choice to save Rocky when he knew he wouldn’t be able to go home if he did.

While trust in the government is certainly at an all time low right now, I don’t think it’s fair to compare that state to what Stratt did to Grace, if for no other reason than in the world that is laid out in PHM the audience understands the necessity of Grace going on the mission to save humanity, there are no other options. What Stratt did was logical and necessary and even Grace came to see that. Now if we had been told a different story where it turned out that Stratt was corrupt or unreliable or something then maybe you might see more discussion on this matter.
6. The reason it’s a powerful scene and it’s the right placement is that it totally recontextualizes his bravery throughout the ship portion of the film. Meaning, it reinforces a key theme that bravery is what you do, not who you are. The powerful juxtaposition of his weakness and selfishness before the voyage and his selflessness and bravery during the voyage is probably the strongest part of the movie thematically. 

I also rate it a 7/10.  Good popcorn film, well cast, strong acting. Not a classic. 
7. haven't seen the film yet but it sounds pretty faithful to the source material from what i've read/seen online. the book depicts his refusal of the mission as more of "cowardice" and maybe a bit of complacency imo. it really isn't interested in going into the political side of things. his action to go back and save Rocky is presented as a personal growth and triumph for him. IIRC, the reveal happens close to the part where he goes to save Rocky, likely to contrast his "cowardice" with new found bravery and selflessness he demonstrates in the face of certain death.
